title: Rett Syndrome
chief_complaint: My daughter was developing normally - she was sitting, babbling, and starting to say 'mama' and 'dada'. Around 12 months she stopped babbling, stopped reaching for toys, and started wringing her hands constantly. Her head growth has slowed. She seems to have lost skills she once had.

physical_exam.Neurologic: Hand-wringing stereotypies (repetitive, midline hand movements - pathognomonic). Bruxism (teeth grinding). Microcephaly (head circumference 3rd percentile, was 50th at 6 months).
physical_exam.Development: Lost purposeful hand use (now stereotyped). No speech (lost previous babble). Social withdrawal (autistic-like features but with intense eye gaze - 'Rett gaze'). Unable to walk independently (regressed).
physical_exam.Growth: Deceleration of head growth. Poor weight gain.
physical_exam.Breathing: Episodes of hyperventilation alternating with breath-holding while awake (autonomic dysfunction).
physical_exam.Musculoskeletal: Hypotonia progressing to spasticity. Scoliosis developing.

hidden_data.genetic: MECP2 gene mutation (pathogenic variant - R168X). De novo (not inherited from parents).
hidden_data.eeg: Disorganized background with multifocal epileptiform discharges. Clinical seizures not observed but high risk.
hidden_data.mri_brain: Diffuse cerebral atrophy. No structural lesion.
hidden_data.echo: Normal cardiac function. Prolonged QTc (520ms) - common in Rett (monitor).

correct_diagnosis: Rett Syndrome (Classic) - MECP2 Mutation, Stage II (Rapid Destructive Phase)
key_learning_points: Rett syndrome = severe neurodevelopmental disorder from MECP2 mutation. X-linked dominant. Affects females almost exclusively (males with MECP2 usually die in utero or have severe neonatal encephalopathy)

Classic stages: Stage I (6-18 months): Developmental arrest, decelerating head growth. Stage II (1-4 years): RAPID regression - loss of hand skills, speech, social engagement. Hand-wringing stereotypies emerge. Stage III (2-10 years): Pseudostationary - seizures, breathing abnormalities, bruxism. Stage IV (>10 years): Late motor deterioration - scoliosis, rigidity, cachexia
Pathognomonic: Hand-wringing (or hand-to-mouth) stereotypies + Developmental regression + Microcephaly + Loss of purposeful hand use + Language loss
Diagnosis: CLINICAL + MECP2 genetic testing. MECP2 positive in 95% of classic Rett. Atypical Rett with CDKL5 or FOXG1 mutations

Treatment: SUPPORTIVE. Physical/occupational/speech therapy. AEDs for seizures (Valproate, Levetiracetam). Beta-blockers for prolonged QTc. Treat scoliosis (bracing → surgery). Nutrition support (feeding difficulties, growth failure). Life expectancy: Survive to adulthood with good care (median survival 50+ years)
